750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}

814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}

825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[

831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}

837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}

877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).

897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

25	\section{Setup and motivation}
26	
27	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
28	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
29	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
30	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
31	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
33	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
34	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
35	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
36	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
37	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
38	
39	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
40	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
41	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
42	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
43	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
44	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
45	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
46	\[
47	  \begin{array}{ccc}
48	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
49	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
50	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
51	  \end{array}
52	\]
53	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
54	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
56	
57	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}

70	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
71	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
72	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
73	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
74	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
75	  % the unit ideal, see
76	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
77	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
78	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
79	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
80	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
81	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
82	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
83	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
84	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
85	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
86	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
87	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
88	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
89	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
90	  \[
91	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
92	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
93	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
94	  \]
95	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
96	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
97	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
98	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
99	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
100	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
101	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
102	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
103	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
104	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
105	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
106	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
107	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
108	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
109	\end{definition}

111	\begin{definition}
112	
113	\leanok
114	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
115	  \label{def:cech_complex}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
117	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
118	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
119	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
120	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
121	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
122	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
123	  % is identified with the complex
124	  % $$
125	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
126	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
127	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
128	  % \ldots
129	  % $$
130	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
131	  % \begin{equation}
132	  % \label{equation-extended}
133	  % 0 \to
134	  % M \to
135	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
136	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
137	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
138	  % \ldots
139	  % \end{equation}
140	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
141	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
142	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
143	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
144	  vanishing).}
145	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
146	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
147	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
148	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
149	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
150	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
151	  in degree \(p\) it is
152	  \[
153	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
154	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
155	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
156	  \]
157	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
158	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
159	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
160	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
161	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
162	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
163	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
164	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
165	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
166	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
167	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
168	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
169	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
170	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
171	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
172	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
176	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
177	
178	It is convenient to factor the construction of
179	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
180	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
181	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
182	
183	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
184	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
185	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
186	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
187	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
188	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
189	\[
190	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
191	    \;=\;
192	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
193	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
194	\]
195	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
196	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
197	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
198	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
199	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
200	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of

200	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
201	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
202	
203	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
204	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
205	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
206	\[
207	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
208	  \qquad
209	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
210	\]
211	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
212	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
213	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
214	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
215	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
216	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
217	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
218	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
219	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
220	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
221	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
222	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
223	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
224	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
225	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
226	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
227	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
228	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
229	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
230	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
231	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
232	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
233	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
234	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
235	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
236	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
237	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
238	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
239	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
240	
241	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
242	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
243	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
244	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
245	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
246	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
247	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
248	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
249	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
250	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
251	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
252	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
253	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
254	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
255	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
256	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
257	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
258	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
259	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
260	
261	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
262	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
263	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
264	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
265	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
266	
267	\begin{definition}\leanok
268	[Arrow of an affine cover]
269	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
271	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
272	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
273	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
274	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
275	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
276	  apply.
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
280	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
282	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
283	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
284	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
285	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
286	  \[
287	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
288	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
289	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
290	  \]
291	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
292	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
293	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
294	\end{definition}
295	
296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull object map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
300	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
301	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
302	  \[
303	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
304	  \]
305	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
306	\end{definition}
307	
308	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
309	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
310	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
311	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
312	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
313	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
314	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
315	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
316	  \[
317	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
318	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
319	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
320	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
321	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
322	      \;=\;
323	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
324	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
325	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
326	  \]
327	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
328	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
329	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
330	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
331	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
332	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
333	\end{definition}
334	
335	\begin{lemma}
336	
337	\leanok
338	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
339	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
341	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
342	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
343	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{proof}
347	
348	\leanok
349	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
350	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
351	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
352	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
353	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
354	\end{proof}
355	
356	\begin{lemma}
357	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
358	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
360	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
361	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
362	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
363	  \[
364	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
365	  \]
366	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
367	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
368	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
369	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
370	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
371	\end{lemma}
372	
373	\begin{proof}
374	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
375	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
376	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
377	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
378	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
379	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
380	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
381	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
382	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
383	\end{proof}
384	
385	\begin{lemma}
386	
387	\leanok
388	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
389	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
390	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
391	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
392	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
393	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
394	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
395	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
396	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
397	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
398	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
399	  \[
400	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
401	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
402	    \;=\;
403	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
404	  \]
405	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
406	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
407	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
408	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
409	\end{lemma}
410	
411	\begin{proof}
412	
413	\leanok
414	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
415	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
416	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}
420	
421	\leanok
422	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
423	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
424	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
425	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
426	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
427	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
429	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
430	  collapses to the transport-light form
431	  \[
432	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_2
434	    \;=\;
435	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
436	      \;\circ\; e_3,
437	  \]
438	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
439	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
440	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
441	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
442	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
443	  composition (pentagon) law.
444	\end{lemma}
445	
446	\begin{proof}
447	
448	\leanok
449	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
450	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
451	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
452	\end{proof}
453	
454	\begin{definition}
455	
456	\leanok
457	[Push--pull functor]
458	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
460	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
461	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
462	  \[
463	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
464	  \]
465	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
466	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
467	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
468	  laws established above: the identity law

507	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
508	
509	\begin{definition}
510	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
511	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
513	  \mathlibok
514	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
515	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
516	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
517	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
518	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
519	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
520	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
521	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
522	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
523	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
524	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
525	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
526	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
527	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
528	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
529	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
530	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
531	\end{definition}
532	
533	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
534	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
535	
536	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
537	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
538	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
539	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
540	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
541	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
542	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
543	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
544	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
545	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
546	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
548	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
549	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
550	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
551	
552	\begin{definition}
553	
554	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
555	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}

567	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
568	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
569	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
570	  % as an $R_f$-module.
571	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
572	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
573	  % are the maps
574	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
575	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
576	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
577	  items (4)--(5).}
578	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
579	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
580	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
581	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
582	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
583	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
584	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
585	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
586	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
587	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
588	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
589	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
590	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
591	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
592	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
593	
594	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
595	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
596	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
597	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
598	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
599	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
600	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
601	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
602	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
603	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
604	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
605	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
606	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
607	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
608	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
609	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
610	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
611	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
612	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
613	
614	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
615	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
616	  intersection identity
617	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
618	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
619	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
620	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
621	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
622	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
623	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
624	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
625	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
626	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
627	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
628	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
629	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
630	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
631	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
632	
633	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
634	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
635	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
636	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
637	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
638	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
639	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
640	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
641	  restriction map through these comparisons.
642	\end{definition}

671	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
672	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_ab_exact}
673	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
674	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (the {\v C}ech complex is
675	  % the complex of localisations) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
676	  % L58--67).
677	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
678	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
679	  % is identified with the complex
680	  % $$
681	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
682	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
683	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
684	  % \ldots
685	  % $$"
686	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
687	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
688	  complex of localisations).}
689	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
690	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
691	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
692	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
693	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
694	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
695	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
696	  \[
697	    D^\bullet : \quad
698	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
699	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
700	    \cdots,
701	    \qquad
702	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
703	  \]
704	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
705	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
706	  \[
707	    1 \leq p
708	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
709	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
710	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
711	  \]
712	  which is the form the affine vanishing
713	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
714	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
715	  homomorphisms,
716	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
717	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
718	
719	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
720	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
721	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
722	  product}
723	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
724	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
725	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
726	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
727	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
728	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
729	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
730	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
731	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
732	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
733	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
734	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
735	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
736	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
737	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
738	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
739	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
740	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
742	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
743	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
744	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
745	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
746	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
747	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
748	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
749	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
750	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
751	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
752	\end{lemma}
753	
754	\begin{proof}
755	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
756	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
757	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
758	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
759	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
760	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
761	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
762	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
763	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
764	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
765	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
766	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
767	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
768	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
769	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
770	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
771	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
772	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
773	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
774	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
775	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
776	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
777	  element-level product equivalence
778	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
779	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
780	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
781	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
782	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
783	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
784	
785	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
786	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
787	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
788	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
789	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
790	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
791	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
792	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
793	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
794	  being proved.
795	\end{proof}

808	  an equivalence of underlying types

811	    \;\simeq\;
812	    \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma),
813	  \]
814	  between the elements of the categorical product \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma} F_\sigma\) in
815	  \(\mathrm{Ab}\) and the dependent functions \(\sigma \mapsto\) (element of
816	  \(F_\sigma\)). It is supplied by the Mathlib concrete-limit comparison
817	  \(\operatorname{CategoryTheory.Limits.Concrete.productEquiv}\), which holds because the
818	  forgetful functor \(\operatorname{forget}\,\mathrm{Ab}\) preserves the discrete
819	  product (\(\operatorname{PreservesLimit}(\operatorname{Discrete.functor} F)\)). Under
820	  this equivalence each coordinate

825	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} (both

827	  the comparison is the canonical isomorphism). This is the move between the

830	  ladder transport in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact} --- is recorded as
831	  \(\operatorname{sectionProdAddEquiv}\), the \(\operatorname{AddEquiv}\) underlying
832	  the coordinatewise comparison.

834	  definitional restatement

836	  image of \(y\) under the equivalence is the underlying group map of the categorical

895	  abstract shape that the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of

903	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\): by construction \(\operatorname{objD} q\)
904	  is the alternating sum \(\sum_{i} (-1)^i\, d^i\) of the cosimplicial cofaces, each
905	  coface \(d^i\) being the \(\operatorname{Pi.lift}\) whose \(\sigma\)-component
906	  projects to the coordinate \(\sigma \circ d_i\) and post-composes with the presheaf
907	  restriction \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\). Reading the
908	  \(\sigma\)-coordinate through the product equivalence (the coordinate-projection

916	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) precomposed with the projection to
917	  \(\sigma \circ d_i\). Assembling the signs yields the stated alternating sum.

1022	  reduction and a ladder transport:
1023	  \begin{itemize}

1025	      homology at degree \(q+1\) is equivalent to exactness of the short complex

1040	  the two consecutive coface differentials \(d^q\), \(d^{q+1}\) of the section

1148	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1149	  \[

1151	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).

1154	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section

1158	\begin{proof}
1159	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1160	  \leanok
1161	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1162	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1163	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1164	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1165	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1166	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1167	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1168	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1169	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1170	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1171	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that

1211	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
1212	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
1213	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$ be a standard
1214	  % open covering of an affine open of $X$.

1217	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
1218	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
1219	  vanishing).}
1220	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) the associated
1221	  affine scheme, and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
1222	  (\(\mathcal{F} : (\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Modules}\)). Let
1223	  \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) be a spanning family of the unit ideal,
1224	  \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\), and let
1225	  \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in \iota} D(s_i)\) be the associated standard
1226	  affine cover of Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}; the index set and the
1227	  affine pieces \(D(s_i)\) with sections \(R_{s_i}\) are read off directly from the
1228	  spanning family. The statement is \emph{intrinsic} to \(\operatorname{Spec} R\):
1229	  there is no relative morphism \(f\) and no pushforward in the conclusion. Then the
1230	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1231	  \mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- the cochain
1232	  complex of abelian groups whose degree-\(p\) term is
1233	  \(\prod_{\sigma}\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) --- has vanishing cohomology in all
1234	  positive degrees:
1235	  \[
1236	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } p > 0,
1237	  \]
1238	  where \(\check{H}^p\) denotes the cohomology of the section complex
1239	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\). Equivalently, the
1240	  extended complex
1241	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}}
1242	  \to \cdots\) of localisations of \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
1243	  is exact in positive degrees. This is the standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing
1244	  only; the sheaf-cohomology Serre vanishing \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) is the
1245	  separate Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} below, obtained from this complex
1246	  vanishing by the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}.
1247	\end{lemma}
1248	
1249	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Write $U = \Spec(A)$ for some ring $A$.
1250	% In other words, $f_1, \ldots, f_n$ are elements of $A$
1251	% which generate the unit ideal of $A$.
1252	% Write $\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}$ for some $A$-module $M$.
1253	% ...

1257	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
1258	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.

1260	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. ...
1261	% Let us define a homotopy
1262	% $$
1263	% h :
1264	% \prod\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}}
1265	% M_{f_{i_0} \ldots f_{i_{p + 1}}, \mathfrak p}

1276	\begin{proof}
1277	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:qcoh_sections_localized, lem:section_cech_homology_exact, lem:section_cech_module_exact, lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_coface_match}
1278	  Write \(R\) for the ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for the
1279	  global sections, and \((s : \iota \to R,\; hs : \operatorname{Ideal.span}
1280	  (\operatorname{range} s) = \top)\) for the spanning family of
1281	  Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}.
1282	
1283	  \paragraph{Reduction to the localised complex.}
1284	  By the section identification of Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, each
1285	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap D(s_{\sigma(p)})\)
1286	  is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
1287	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)}\), and its sections are the away localisation
1288	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma)) = M_{s_\sigma}\); under these identifications each face
1289	  restriction is the canonical localisation map. Hence
1290	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} identifies the section {\v C}ech complex
1291	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1292	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} with the concrete complex of localised
1293	  \(R\)-modules
1294	  \[
1295	    \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \;\to\;
1296	    \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1}} \;\to\;
1297	    \prod_{i_0 i_1 i_2} M_{s_{i_0} s_{i_1} s_{i_2}} \;\to\; \cdots,
1298	  \]
1299	  and reduces the vanishing
1300	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1301	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\) for \(p > 0\) to positive-degree
1302	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying localisation maps. Equivalently,
1303	  the extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
1304	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is
1305	  exact in positive degrees. The active target is the tilde case
1306	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), where the section identification of
1307	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} is gap-free and the coface match is
1308	  the abstract unfold of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} composed with the
1309	  tilde bridge of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}; a general quasi-coherent
1310	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case by \(\mathcal{F} \cong
1311	  \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the separately-deferred
1312	  globalisation gap.
1313	
1314	  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}
1315	  Exactness of each positive-degree node is established from the spanning family
1316	  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
1317	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)
1318	  reduces \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p-1}, d^{p})\) to exactness after
1319	  localising at each spanning element \(r\) (the away localisation
1320	  \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)), checking exactness one localisation at a time. In the
1321	  localisation at \(s_r\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes \(s_r\)
1322	  invertible, so the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport \(c\)
1323	  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
1324	  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
1325	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
1326	  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
1327	  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
1328	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
1329	  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
1330	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
1331	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
1332	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
1333	  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
1334	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
1335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
1336	  abstract section complex.
1337	
1338	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1339	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1340	  extra-degeneracy packaging does not apply here (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map
1341	  is only a section-level homotopy after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra
1342	  degeneracy), so the concrete
1343	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1344	  is used instead.
1345	\end{proof}
1346	
1347	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1348	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1349	
1350	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1351	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1352	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1353	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1354	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1355	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1356	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1357	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1358	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1359	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1360	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1361	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1362	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1363	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1364	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1365	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1366	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1367	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1368	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1369	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1370	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1371	
1372	\begin{definition}
1373	
1374	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1375	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1377	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,

1379	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,

1381	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,

1389	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1390	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1391	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1392	  % L1095--1135).
1393	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1394	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1395	  % $$
1396	  % \ldots
1397	  % \to
1398	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1399	  % \to
1400	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1401	  % \to
1402	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1403	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1404	  % $$
1405	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1406	  % and where the map
1407	  % $$
1408	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1409	  % \longrightarrow
1410	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1411	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1412	  % $$
1413	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1414	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1415	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1416	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1417	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1418	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1419	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1420	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1421	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1422	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1423	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1424	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1425	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1426	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1427	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1429	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1430	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1431	  \[
1432	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1433	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1434	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1435	  \]
1436	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1437	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1438	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1439	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1440	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1441	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1442	  \[
1443	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1444	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1445	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1446	  \]
1447	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1448	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1449	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1450	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1451	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1452	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1453	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1454	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1455	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1456	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1457	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1458	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1459	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1460	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1461	
1462	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1463	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1464	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1465	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1466	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1467	
1468	  The parallel \texttt{...Fam} declarations pinned above are the cover-agnostic
1469	  raw-finite-family mirror of this construction — indexed by an arbitrary finite
1470	  family \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) of opens carrying \emph{no} covering hypothesis — and
1471	  feed the family form of the {\v C}ech acyclicity lemma
1472	  (\(\operatorname{injective\_cech\_acyclicFam}\)).
1473	\end{definition}

7665	\leanok
7666	[The evaluated augmented {\v C}ech complex is the concrete section {\v C}ech complex]
7667	  \label{lem:cechSection_complex_iso}
7668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechSection_complex_iso}
7669	  % NOTE: (iter-056 prover + lvb-csi) MIS-SPECIFIED — statement is FALSE as written and

7672	  % So D ≅ D' fails (they differ at degree 0 by the sheaf property). The correct target is
7673	  % D ≅ D'_aug := (sectionCechComplex …).augment ε hε, where ε : Γ(V,F) → ∏_i Γ(U_i∩V,F) is the
7674	  % restriction product and hε : ε ≫ d⁰ = 0. Blueprint-writer must re-state before re-dispatch.
7675	  \uses{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
7676	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, def:cech_augmented_complex}
7677	  Fix an open \(V \subseteq X\). The augmented {\v C}ech complex of
7678	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmented_complex} evaluated at \(V\) (through
7679	  \(\operatorname{toPresheaf}\)) --- call it \(D^\bullet\), the cochain complex of abelian
7680	  groups with \(D^p = \Gamma(V, \operatorname{pushPullObj}\mathcal{F}\,Y_p)\) --- is
7681	  isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to the concrete section {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\)
7682	  over the family \(\bigl(\operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,\cdot \cap V\bigr)\): the
7683	  complex with \(D'^p = \prod_\sigma \Gamma(U_\sigma \cap V, \mathcal{F})\) and the
7684	  alternating coface restriction differential. The isomorphism is the degreewise map of
7685	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso}, and it intertwines the two differentials.
7686	\end{lemma}
7687	\begin{proof}
7688	  \uses{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso, lem:section_cech_objd_apply,
7689	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
7690	  In each degree \(p\) the object isomorphism \(D^p \cong D'^p\) is
7691	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_eval_prod_iso}. It remains to check the degreewise isomorphisms
7692	  commute with the differentials. The differential of \(D'^\bullet\) is, read through the
7693	  product equivalence \(\operatorname{sectionCechProductEquiv}\)
7694	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), the alternating sum of presheaf face
7695	  restrictions \(\sum_i (-1)^i\, \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)\) computed in
7696	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}. The differential of \(D^\bullet\) is the
7697	  evaluation at \(V\) of the {\v C}ech-nerve coface maps, assembled from the same push--pull
7698	  restriction comparisons; under the degreewise identification each coface of \(D^\bullet\)
7699	  becomes the corresponding face restriction of \(D'^\bullet\), so the alternating sums
7700	  agree. Reusing the alternating-sum bookkeeping of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply}
7701	  --- rather than rebuilding it --- matches the differentials and yields the isomorphism of
7702	  complexes.
7703	\end{proof}

7718	    def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
7719	  Suppose \(V \le \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) for some fixed index
7720	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), i.e.\ \(V\) lies inside one cover member. Then the concrete section
7721	  {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso} over the family
7722	  \(U'_j = \operatorname{coverInterOpen}\mathcal{U}\,j \cap V\) admits a contracting homotopy
7723	  \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet} \simeq 0\).
7724	\end{lemma}
7725	\begin{proof}
7726	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:cech_engine_complex}
7727	  Because \(V \le U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}}\), the restricted family \(U'_j = U_j \cap V\) has a
7728	  \emph{maximum}: \(U'_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} = U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \cap V = V\), and every
7729	  \(U'_j \le V\). Consequently, for each multi-index \(\sigma\),
7730	  \(U'_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}\,\sigma} = U_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}} \cap U_\sigma \cap V = U_\sigma \cap
7731	  V = U'_\sigma\): prepending the index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) does not change the open, so the
7732	  prepend map is the \emph{identity} on each coefficient \(\Gamma(U'_\sigma, \mathcal{F})\)
7733	  (the membership criterion \(\operatorname{le\_coverInterOpen\_iff}\) of
7734	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_engine_complex}). The dependent-coefficient combinatorial {\v C}ech
7735	  engine --- \(\operatorname{depDiff}\), \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\),
7736	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy\_spec}\), \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of
7737	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (here Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is cited only as
7738	  the Lean home of the \(\operatorname{dep\ast}\) engine declarations --- its standard-cover {\v
7739	  C}ech-vanishing \emph{conclusion} is \emph{not} a mathematical dependency of the present
7740	  contractibility argument) --- then supplies the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\)
7741	  contracting homotopy \(h\) with \(d \circ h + h \circ d = \mathrm{id}\), since its unit,
7742	  shift, and coface-commutation compatibilities all hold with the prepend map acting as the
7743	  identity. Packaging \(h\) as a homotopy of cochain complexes gives \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet}

7747	\begin{lemma}
7748	[Local vanishing of the evaluated augmented {\v C}ech homology]
7749	  \label{lem:cechSection_isZero_homology}
7750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechSection_isZero_homology}
7751	  \uses{lem:cechSection_complex_iso, lem:cechSection_contractible,
7752	    lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7753	  Let \(V \le \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i\) for some index \(i\). Then for every
7754	  degree \(p\) the homology \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) of the augmented {\v C}ech complex evaluated at
7755	  \(V\) (the complex \(D^\bullet\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso}) is a zero
7756	  object; equivalently \(D^\bullet\) carries a contracting homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}
7757	  \simeq 0\). This is the residual obligation of Step~3 of
7758	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}.
7759	\end{lemma}
7760	\begin{proof}
7761	  \uses{lem:cechSection_complex_iso, lem:cechSection_contractible,
7762	    lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero}
7763	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_complex_iso} the evaluated complex \(D^\bullet\) is
7764	  isomorphic to the concrete section {\v C}ech complex \(D'^\bullet\). The hypothesis \(V \le
7765	  \operatorname{coverOpen}\mathcal{U}\,i\) places \(V\) inside one cover member, so
7766	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cechSection_contractible} gives a contracting homotopy \(\mathrm{id}_{D'^\bullet}
7767	  \simeq 0\); transporting it across the complex isomorphism yields \(\mathrm{id}_{D^\bullet}
7768	  \simeq 0\). Lemma~\ref{lem:isZero_homology_of_homotopy_id_zero} then makes
7769	  \(H^p(D^\bullet)\) a zero object in every degree.
7770	\end{proof}

7774	\begin{lemma}[Pushforward sections are sections over the preimage]
7775	  \label{lem:pushforward_obj_obj_mathlib}
7776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pushforward_obj_obj}
7777	  \mathlibok
7778	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
7779	  For a morphism \(f : X \to Y\), an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), and an open
7780	  \(U \subseteq Y\), the sections of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{M}\) over \(U\) coincide
7781	  definitionally with the sections of \(\mathcal{M}\) over the preimage:
7782	  \(\Gamma(U, f_*\mathcal{M}) = \Gamma(f^{-1}(U), \mathcal{M})\).
7783	\end{lemma}
7784	
7785	\begin{lemma}[Pullback along an open immersion is the restriction functor]
7786	  \label{lem:restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
7787	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}
7788	  \mathlibok
7789	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
7790	  For an open immersion \(f : X \to Y\), the restriction functor
7791	  \(\operatorname{restrictFunctor} f : Y.\mathrm{Modules} \to X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is
7792	  naturally isomorphic to the module pullback functor \(f^* = \operatorname{pullback} f\). In
7793	  particular \(f^*\mathcal{M} \cong \mathcal{M}.\operatorname{restrict} f\) for every
7794	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\).
7795	\end{lemma}

7797	\begin{lemma}[Evaluation of a sheaf of modules preserves products]
7798	  \label{lem:evaluation_preserves_products_mathlib}
7799	  \lean{SheafOfModules.evaluationPreservesLimitsOfShape}
7800	  \mathlibok
7801	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
7802	  For a sheaf of rings on a site and any object \(U\), the evaluation (sections-at-\(U\))
7803	  functor \(\operatorname{evaluation}\,U : \operatorname{SheafOfModules} \to \mathrm{Ab}\)
7804	  preserves limits of every shape; in particular it preserves products, so \(\Gamma(U,
7805	  \prod_\sigma N_\sigma) \cong \prod_\sigma \Gamma(U, N_\sigma)\).
7806	\end{lemma}

7808	\begin{lemma}[Sections over a binary coproduct scheme are a product]
7809	  \label{lem:coprodPresheafObjIso_mathlib}
7810	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.coprodPresheafObjIso}
7811	  \mathlibok
7812	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
7813	  For schemes \(X, Y\) and an open \(U\) of the coproduct \(X \amalg Y\), the structure-sheaf
7814	  sections over \(U\) decompose as the product of the sections over the two preimages:
7815	  \((X \amalg Y).\mathcal{O}(U) \cong X.\mathcal{O}(\operatorname{inl}^{-1} U) \times
7816	  Y.\mathcal{O}(\operatorname{inr}^{-1} U)\). This is the binary disjoint-union sheaf
7817	  decomposition that Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_sigma_iso} ports to the indexed,
7818	  \(\operatorname{SheafOfModules}\) setting.
7819	\end{lemma}
7820	
7821	\begin{lemma}[Sections of a sheaf over a disjoint union are a product]
7822	  \label{lem:isProductOfDisjoint_mathlib}
7823	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.isProductOfDisjoint}
7824	  \mathlibok
7825	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
7826	  For a sheaf \(F\) (valued in a category with products) on a topological space and two opens
7827	  \(U, V\) with \(U \sqcap V = \bot\) (disjoint), the sections over the union are the product
7828	  of the sections: \(F(U \sqcup V) \cong F(U) \times F(V)\). Iterated over a finite family,
7829	  this is the disjoint-union decomposition underlying Lemma~\ref{lem:pushPull_sigma_iso}.
7830	\end{lemma}
7831

7833	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
7834	  \label{lem:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}

7842	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
7843	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
7844	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
7845	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
7846	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
7847	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
7848	  \[
7849	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)

8270	  isomorphism \(F \cong G\) yields two derived natural transformations, and the composition and
8271	  identity compatibilities show they are mutually inverse; hence
8272	  \(F.\mathrm{rightDerived}\,n \cong G.\mathrm{rightDerived}\,n\).

8275	\begin{lemma}[The canonical isomorphism of an affine scheme with its spectrum]
8276	  \label{lem:isoSpec_scheme_mathlib}
8277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isoSpec}
8278	  \mathlibok
8279	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
8280	  Let \(U\) be a scheme with \([\,\mathrm{IsAffine}\ U\,]\). Then there is a canonical
8281	  \emph{whole-scheme} isomorphism of schemes
8282	  \[
8283	    U \;\cong\; \operatorname{Spec}\Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_U),
8284	  \]
8285	  identifying \(U\) with the spectrum of its ring of global sections. This is a genuine isomorphism of
8286	  schemes (not merely an open immersion), so it induces an equivalence of module categories.
